desc:
The broad once-beautiful avenue bends from south to east, flanked on the outside
of the curve by an ornamental marble colonnade. Behind the columns there was
once a monumental bas-relief. Someone has taken an exceptional dislike to this
work; it has been systematically chiselled away, and marble chips litter the
floor of the tunnel.
name: Curving Underground Avenue

desc:
The underground avenue identified as Durin's Way makes a broad bend to the
north, from the point of view of an eastbound traveller. East of the bend is a
squarish side chamber parallel to the road, possibly a siding for the unloading
of freight. Some large stone bins are here with runners on the bottoms. In the
east wall are four stone openings large enough for the bins to fit within.
name: Bend in Durin's Way

desc:
A broad underground road makes a turn here from westward to northward. The walls
have been much battered and pitted, but it seems that some mosaics showing the
industry of the dwarves and the victories they had won with their axes still
remain visible on the tunnel's walls.
name: Bend in an Underground Road

desc:
This is the end of an underground road which comes from the south and stops here
in a square chamber, broad though low, with a strong-looking metal door in the
north wall. The four corners of the chamber bear lamps mounted on struts which
lean toward the centre. A plain corridor departs westward.
name: Underground Square

desc:
The level corridor continues straight east and west through the mountain. Above
you there is a great hole in the ceiling; not the result of a collapse, but a
tunnel of the same dimensions as this one, which seems to ascend straight
upwards. The air that blows down from above is cooler, but it bears a mixture of
pungent animal smells.
name: Narrow Way

desc:
This tunnel is like a narrow corridor which has been delved through the stone in
an east-west direction. Its walls, floor, and ceiling are flat and meet at
precise right angles, but there is little ornamentation. Perhaps it was awaiting
further work when the dwarves were driven from Khazad-dum long ago.
name: Narrow Way

desc:
You are in a draughty, high-ceilinged chamber where a passage from the west
divides into three eastward-trending ways. To the left, a wide road descends a
ramp; to the right, a similar road ascends into blackness. Connecting the upward
and downward paths is a curved patch of stone floor which was actually worn down
about two inches by the traffic long ago. Between the two, a narrower level
passage proceeds straight eastward. On the walls are mounted some lamps in
pairs. This was apparently a well- travelled junction once. There is a low
archway in the north wall of the chamber.
name: Intersection of Roads

desc:
A deep fissure has opened in the rock here, perpendicular to the direction of
the tunnel. In the ceiling it is a noticeable crack; on the walls it is about a
foot wide. Where it crosses the path you are on, it makes a four- foot gap.
However, at the moment the gap is bridged by a huge flat stone slab which
someone has placed across it.
name: Level Tunnel

desc:
The road continues eastward into the darkness. On the north and south the way is
marked by the openings of ore-chutes, by uninviting stairways, and by black
doorways whose doors swing vacantly wide. Cracks are beginning to appear in the
walls and floor, and every so often a chunk of the roadway is found to have
collapsed inward into some vacancy of unknown depth.
name: Level Tunnel

desc:
A circular chamber about forty feet across with a central stone pier has been
excavated at the intersection of four ways. The floors of the circle and of the
four tunnels are very flat and coated with dried mud. The tunnels leading
outward are about sixteen feet in width; about two feet above the floor narrow
walkways have been cut on either side.
name: Intersection of Four Ways

desc:
The east-west passage continues as before. On the north and south sides are some
bays or sidings, some of which contain stone bins or notches for partitions. To
the east, the passageway opens out into a large intersection.
name: East-West Tunnel

desc:
The east-west tunnel continues through the stone of the mountain, ascending to
the west, descending to the east. Here and there the roof of the tunnel is
marked by the black opening of a vent or chute. Stairways, ladders, and doorways
are frequently encountered, but in this part of the tunnel they are usually
blocked or damaged.
name: East-West Tunnel

desc:
An east-west tunnel is hewn through the solid stone here. Its walls and ceiling
are unadorned; its floor is caked with dirt. At irregular intervals, the mouths
of smaller tunnels open to either side of the main passage. The air is
soundless, warm and oppressive.
name: East-West Tunnel

desc:
You are following an underground road or tunnel which winds through the rock in
great sloping bends. North of here the course is generally upward; to the east,
downward. Here and there great bays open in the rock, in which large lampposts
stand, stone benches, offer rest to the traveller, and doors and windows signal
the presence of an inn, an eating-place, a public store. Now all are of course
abandoned. In the lightless gloom the bays are silent and watchful, and the
absolute blackness of the doors and windows is like that of the eyes of skulls.
name: A Bend in the Underground Road

desc:
You have arrived at a broad plaza whose surface is formed of heavy hexagonal
stone slabs - white, black, and pink marble, greenish gabbro, and other types
you do not recognise. A white marble stairway, the width of the entire plaza,
descends to the west. At the top of the stair are two damaged statues. To the
north and east you dimly perceive what seem to be white trees. Above them you
see some carvings on the walls which rise out of sight in great vaulting curves,
skilfully designed to bear the unimaginable weight of the mountain which rises
far above you.
name: The Ruined Plaza

desc:
You are on a monumental stairway, two hundred feet wide, which ascends eastward.
The steps are strewn with rubble. You cannot make out a landing either below or
above, no matter how hard you peer into the darkness. A great ornamental window
can be dimly seen above you to the west. The north and south walls are lined
with rune-carved panels, with sconces above them; you also notice some odd holes
in the walls.
name: On the Great Stairway

desc:
This is a great, once-magnificent entrance hall, whose farthest reaches are
shrouded in impenetrable blackness. Sconces and lamps, unused now for centuries,
can be dimly seen on the walls nearest to you. In the west wall are the great
portals of the Doors of Durin. Opposite them, across a broad marble floor, a
wide stairway rises into the gloom and vanishes. The air is still and stale; the
echoes of your footfalls are weak, and all sounds are hushed by the immensities
of space and age. Once, long ago, this hall was well-lit, festive, and
companionable, a meeting-place for elves and dwarves and the gateway to the
glory of Khazad-dum. Today it is dark, bleak, and watchful. Some bones lie
scattered against the base of the north wall.
name: Inside Durin's Gate

desc:
You are where a broad underground road widens into an underground chamber, whose
roof is supported by a line of square pillars on either side of the roadway. The
edges of the chamber are lined with low loading platforms and freight docks.
Beyond them, in the darkness the black doorways of vacant looted warehouses can
be vaguely seen.
name: Cargo Road

desc:
The ceiling soars upward and the pillars lining the road disappear in the gloom
above, as the road passes directly through a huge underground room - obviously a
warehouse. The upper levels are wider than the lower ones, and a ramp spirals
upward along the west, north, and east wall, reaching the top level just above
the east entrance. In the great days of the Dwarves, this warehouse was full of
goods received from all the peoples of the world. Now it is empty, still, and
black.
name: Warehouse

desc:
This road is surfaced with a very dark stone, black flecked with green. So are
the walls up to about the height of a dwarf. The ceiling is covered with small
rectangular tiles of a much lighter stone. Other than floor and ceiling, there
is not much to see in this tunnel, which slopes downward to the south.
name: Dark Road

desc:
The dark-surfaced road slopes upward to the north and downward to the south.
Water drips from around some of the ceiling tiles; some have become detached,
revealing wet cracked stone. The drips form a rivulet which runs down the base
of the east wall of the tunnel, meandering and depositing a layer of silt. There
seem to be some bipedal tracks there, but the silt does not hold the outline
well.
name: Dark Road

desc:
The dark road bends here, turning left in its downward course. The floor of the
tunnel has become coated with silt and slime and is quite slippery. The stones
of the walls are also wet. Clearly this location is partially submerged from
time to time.
name: Bend in the Dark Road

desc:
Here a dark-surfaced underground road comes to an abrupt end at the shore of a
subterranean lake - at least that is your first impression. In fact, the
pavement continues eastward beneath the surface, dropping rapidly off into
unknown depths. A great void has opened up above you. Perhaps the cave-in that
created this cavern also blocked the drainage somehow so as to form the lake.
name: Edge of an Underground Lake

desc:
This roadway is surfaced with a smooth dark stone, which also lines the walls to
a height of three feet. Above that level the unfinished surface of the hard
stone through which the road is bored arches overhead in broken curves.
name: A Dark Road

desc:
The hard dark-surfaced road bends here, seemingly to avoid a great mass of white
crystalline rock on the northwest side of the passage. From this point it
ascends to the east and descends to the south.
name: A Dark Road

desc:
The road bends here, following the path of a natural crevice in the rock which
scars the roof of the tunnel, zigzagging from one side to the other and back.
The rock here is a dark grey shot through with bright crystalline veins. Some of
the outcroppings of the lighter stone in the walls of the tunnel have been
crudely chipped and dug into.
name: A Dark Road

desc:
The roof and west wall of the tunnel have disappeared. The road hugs the face of
a west-facing precipice; above and westward are only a lightless void. Yet you
are still underground. The wall to your east does not look like part of any
natural cave. There are no natural sounds; all around is black and still. A curb
half a foot high guards the western edge of the roadway.
name: The Mines of Khulakal

desc:
A tremendous cave-in has happened here. A dark-surfaced road arrives from the
south, begins an eastward turn, and at that point disappears completely, running
at an angle into a great rock face. A second look shows that the rock is a
single great slab of the mountain which has fallen from above, obliterating the
road. The precipice to the west is too steep to descend, but there is a footpath
which climbs up to the east and north along the junction between the fallen rock
and the cliff above where the road used to be. The rock here is veined with
blue-white quartz.
name: The Mines of Khulakal

desc:
This path winds over, around, between, and under massive slabs of fallen stone.
Here it leads through a crack between two boulders each the size of a hill.
There it burrows under a great stone through a pile of fine rubble. And there
again the roof opens out exposing you to the awesome blackness overhead, which
might collapse on you at any time, of course.
name: A Partly Buried Path

desc:
A great supporting column of the mine lies here, broken like the stem of a
goblet. Its base juts up just here; the rest of it lies here, extended northward
along a rock face. The column was hewn from the stone by the Dwarves; it is
smoothly tapered and bent to carry and distribute the weight of the shoulder of
the mountain overhead, as if it were an ornamental column in a king's palace.
The base, however, shows signs of a later, less skilful mining operation.
name: The Mines of Khulakal

desc:
You are on a slender, railless, east-west path surrounded by nothingness in all
directions. All around you is a great black vacancy, the central digging of a
Dwarven mine. The path runs along the top of a structural buttress, and is not
designed for ease of travel. Travelling between two elevated points, it makes a
long downward curve, at the low point of which you are.
name: Narrow Walk

desc:
You are at the base of a mighty supporting column in a Dwarven mine. Because of
their peerless science of stone and force, the Khazad were able to delve
incredible caverns such as this within the northwest shoulder of the peak called
Silvertine. The column and the buttresses that lead away from it are carefully
shaped to bear the weight of the rock above and transmit it into the roots of
the mountain. Doubtless they left behind no more ore- bearing rock than they had
to; if anyone were to become greedy and dig more, it would produce a
catastrophe.
name: Base of a Pillar

desc:
A road surfaced with smooth dark stone abruptly becomes impassable here, as it
and the rock supporting it have collapsed into the depths below. The road may be
followed back to the east, and a plain side road follows a spur of the walls
southward. Surrounding you is the oppressive and threatening blackness of the
interior of the mountain.
name: Dark Road

desc:
You are at the working face of the Mine of Khulakal. Shallow tunnels are dug
into the stone. Some are precisely hewn in Dwarvish fashion for part of their
length, then become clumsy and misshapen. The ground drops off sharply to the
southwest, heaped with mounds of stone. A narrow road slithers precariously up
the face, northward and eastward. A larger road, surfaced in dark stone, begins
here and goes west along the north wall of the great excavation.
name: The Mines of Khulakal

desc:
This plain stone room was apparently once used for the housing of guards who
watched over the intersection outside. Near the door is a well-mouth surrounded
by a stone curb. The heavy stone lid lies nearby. On the western wall is an
ancient empty weapons-rack, and in the north wall are niches that served as
beds.
name: Guardroom

desc:
A broad dark-surfaced underground road or tunnel runs north and south here. On
either side is a narrow walkway about two feet above the pavement. The roof here
is the native rock of the mountain.
name: Dark Road

desc:
The smooth-surfaced road continues north and south from here, sloping downward
to the north. Here the road is partially blocked by a group of skids which are
piled high with rock. Each skid is about fourteen feet long and eight feet wide,
and they have clearly been in their present position for a very long time. On
the west side of the tunnel is the open mouth of a chute of some kind which
leads down from above.
name: Dark Smooth Road

desc:
This is a steep, somewhat insecure path over rubble which has fallen from the
roof of the great mine cavern. Despite the fact that it does not obviously go
anywhere important, it has apparently attracted some foot traffic which has worn
a sort of shallow trench. Northwards the path ascends until it reaches some
boulders; southwards it plunges downward into a small tunnel.
name: Well-trodden Passageway

desc:
The road abruptly becomes submerged here as it descends southward. The water is
shallow and calm here, but the pavement drops off quickly. Out of the darkness
to your south you hear the sound of splashing water.
name: Dark Road

desc:
The current of the stream picks up pace here, as it tumbles eastward into the
darkness. However, here there is an opening to the north, where it is possible
to leave the waterway, and return, once more, to the security of solid ground.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
Two waterways clash violently here, whirling and raging all about. The air is
filled with an icy cold spray, and the roar of falling water assails you from
the south, where this torrent gushes over a sharp rocky cliff, into a deep gully
beyond.
name: Meeting of Two Waterways

desc:
The water here is swirling eastward. Occasionally, you bump your head against
the roof of the cave when the water level rises. The stream seems to be
shallower to the east.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
The stream makes a sharp bend here, causing the path to bend likewise. The
water's speed at the bend is so high, that some water splashes against the rocks
and you notice some barnacles growing on the ceiling, suggesting that this
tunnel sometimes gets filled with water.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
Twisting and turning, the underground stream continues its centuries-old task of
eroding the underlying rocks. The air is heavy with moisture from the spray of
the stream as it rushes onwards through the underground tunnel.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
A small underground stream begins its journey from the cold, dark lake, twisting
and bending, and washing rapidly over rocks, slick with moss, and all manner of
other things, unseen beneath the water's surface.
name: Underground Stream

desc:
This is the eastern end of a deep underground lake. The dark mirror-like surface
is occasionally broken by some ripples of water of some creature swimming
beneath the surface. To the south, a small stream begins its journey into the
mountain.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
The icy cold waters of this underground lake surround you. They exude a strange,
unpleasant aroma, at once musky and fishy. The shore is unclimbable here.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
An underground lake extends northward from here. Its dark, heavy, oily waters
lap at a rock wall to the west. Mounds of rubble stick up from the surface. All
is still. Whatever spring fills the lake works quietly, unobtrusively.
name: Underground Lake

desc:
You are at the intersection of two underground roads. One is a dark-surfaced
road, smooth but without much adornment, that leads north and south from here.
The east-west road which crosses it here must have been a major highway in its
time. Its walls are faced with marble, and lined with carvings and ornaments
which someone has done their best to destroy through the centuries.
Lamp-standards rise at the corners of the crossroad.
name: Underground Intersection

desc:
The fissure broadens into an underground chamber about twenty feet wide. North
and south, the chamber narrows to an impassable crevice. Overhead, the fissure
rises into a network of broad cracks and openings, none of which are reachable
at all from where you are. Nearly every surface is caked with pungent guano;
with every step, stinking dust rises up around you and chokes you.
name: Down a Fissure

desc:
Water from the north pitches over a deep descent into this gully, throwing great
sprays of droplets onto the rocky walls of the tunnel. The surface of the water
is white with foam as the torrent twists east and flows rapidly away.
name: Underground Torrent
